record_office_terminology.land_records: Town Clerk (CT records land at the town level, not the county level)
record_office_terminology.probate: Probate Court (CT has ~130 probate districts, not aligned with counties)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Town Clerk (town-level registration from colonial era; statewide registration 1897)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1897 (statewide vital registration); Barbour Collection covers 1640s-1850 from town clerk records

burned_county_authority: Connecticut has no burned counties. Record survival is among the best in the nation due to early town-level record keeping, the Barbour Collection (vital records), the Hale Collection (cemetery records), and the Connecticut State Library's comprehensive microfilming program.
administrative_notes: Connecticut was founded in 1636 (Hartford) and the Fundamental Orders of 1639 are considered the first written constitution. The New Haven Colony (1638-1665) was separate before merging with Connecticut. The original four counties (Hartford, New Haven, New London, Fairfield) were established in 1666. Litchfield (1751), Windham (1726), Middlesex (1785), and Tolland (1785) were created later. CRITICAL: Connecticut abolished county government in 1960 — counties exist only as geographic/judicial boundaries. Town clerks, not county officials, are the primary record keepers for land, vital records, and most other records. Connecticut has approximately 130 probate districts that do NOT align with county boundaries. The Connecticut State Library (CSL) in Hartford is the central genealogical repository for the entire state.

core_repositories.description: The single most important genealogical repository in Connecticut. Houses the Barbour Collection (vital records for 137 towns through 1850), the Hale Collection (cemetery inscriptions and newspaper abstracts), probate records on microfilm, church records, military records, state archives, and town clerk records on microfilm. The History and Genealogy Reading Room is the starting point for all CT research.

Major manuscript repository with family papers, account books, diaries, photographs, and maps. Holds over 4 million manuscripts. The CHS library includes published genealogies, town histories, and the Hoadly transcriptions of Connecticut Colony records.

core_repositories.source_name: New England Historic Genealogical Society (NEHGS) — CT Collections
core_repositories.description: NEHGS holds extensive Connecticut genealogical materials through AmericanAncestors.org. Includes the New England Historical and Genealogical Register (from 1847), CT family genealogies, and digitized manuscript collections. Membership provides access to databases with millions of CT records.

core_repositories.repository: Sterling Memorial Library, Yale University, New Haven
core_repositories.description: Yale holds significant CT manuscript collections including New Haven Colony records, family papers, business records, and church records. The Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library has colonial-era documents. Search the Yale Finding Aids Database (YFAD) before visiting.

familysearch_collections.description: Extensive indexed and browsable collections including probate records (all probate districts through ~1916), vital records, church records, town records, and land records. FamilySearch partnered with CSL to microfilm and digitize thousands of volumes.

Wiki guide to town-level records availability. Connecticut's unique town-clerk-centric system means this guide is essential for identifying which records have been microfilmed for each of CT's 169 towns.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: Library of Congress / Connecticut State Library
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic CT newspapers including the Hartford Courant (oldest continuously published newspaper in America, from 1764), New Haven Register, Bridgeport Standard, New London Day, and dozens of smaller papers. Searchable full text.

CSL's newspaper digitization project. Over 300 CT newspaper titles on microfilm, with ongoing digitization. Hale Collection newspaper abstracts supplement the full-text digitized papers with marriage and death notices not yet digitized.

military_records.description: CSL holds the state's military records from the colonial wars through Vietnam. Includes Adjutant General records, militia rolls, Revolutionary War service records, War of 1812 records, Civil War regimental records and photographs, and WWI/WWII bonus applications. The 'Record of Service of Connecticut Men in the Revolution' (1889) is a key published source.

Digitized military records including CT pension files, service records, and compiled military service records. Revolutionary War pension files are particularly important for CT veterans who migrated to New York, Ohio, and other states.

land_and_property.description: Connecticut land records are filed at the TOWN level, not county level. Most towns now have online access through vendors like IQS or their own portals. Some records date to the 1630s. The CSL has microfilm copies of early town land records for research when town offices are not accessible.

land_and_property.source_name: Connecticut Colony Original Land Grants and Proprietors' Records
land_and_property.repository: Connecticut State Library / Connecticut Historical Society
land_and_property.description: Records of original land divisions by town proprietors from the 1630s forward. The earliest land records predate any formal recording system and are found in town proprietors' meeting minutes. CSL and CHS hold these materials. Published volumes: 'The Public Records of the Colony of Connecticut' (Trumbull, 1850-1890).

vital_records.description: THE foundational CT vital records resource. Barbour abstracted births, marriages, and deaths from town clerk records for 137 Connecticut towns from their founding through approximately 1850. Available on microfilm, in published volumes, and partially digitized on CSL's website. Cross-referenced by name and town.

Statewide vital registration began in 1897. Birth, marriage, and death certificates available from the state with restrictions (births: 100 years, deaths: varies). For pre-1897 records, use the Barbour Collection and individual town clerk offices.

vital_records.source_name: Connecticut Church Record Abstracts (State Vital Records)
vital_records.description: Before town-level civil registration became systematic, church records served as de facto vital records. CSL holds church record abstracts and original church registers deposited by individual parishes — primarily Congregational but also Episcopal, Baptist, Methodist, and Quaker.

church_and_cemetery.description: Charles R. Hale's monumental work: cemetery inscription transcriptions, newspaper marriage and death notices, and church record abstracts. Over 2 million entries covering virtually every CT town. Created 1916-1934 as a WPA-era project. Cards organized by town and name. Partially digitized; full collection available on microfilm at CSL.

Online cemetery documentation for CT. FindAGrave has extensive coverage of CT cemeteries. BillionGraves adds GPS-located photographs. Supplement with the Hale Collection for stones that have deteriorated since the 1920s-1930s survey.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Primary genealogical society for CT. Publishes 'The Connecticut Nutmegger' quarterly journal. Maintains a research library in Glastonbury with local genealogies, town histories, and family files. Offers research assistance and educational programs.

Comprehensive research guide covering CT genealogical resources by topic and town. Includes record-type guides, repository information, and links to digitized collections. Essential starting point for understanding CT's town-level record system.

Genealogical research library with strong CT and New England collections. Offers online databases (American Genealogical-Biographical Index — AGBI) and in-person research facilities. Holds family Bibles, unpublished genealogies, and local history materials.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Connecticut abolished county government effective October 1, 1960. The eight counties (Fairfield, Hartford, Litchfield, Middlesex, New Haven, New London, Tolland, Windham) continue to exist as geographic regions and judicial districts, but have no governmental functions. All county-level services were transferred to the state or to regional planning organizations. No counties have been created, dissolved, or had boundary changes since 1785 (when Middlesex and Tolland were created). Connecticut has never had an extinct county — all eight counties from the 1785 configuration remain as geographic entities.
historical_counties_extinct.notes: Unlike many states, CT has no extinct counties. The Fundamental Orders (1639) established governance at the town level, and towns have always been the primary administrative unit. The county system was always secondary to town government.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Connecticut enacted gradual abolition of slavery in 1784 — children born to enslaved mothers after March 1, 1784 would be free at age 25 (later reduced to 21 in 1797). Town clerks were required to register enslaved persons. CSL holds these registrations. Complete emancipation came in 1848. Records are fragmentary — compliance varied by town.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Witness Stones Project — CT African American History
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Community-based project documenting the lives of enslaved people in Connecticut through historical markers. Research files identify enslaved individuals by name, owner, town, and date. The project's research database is a growing resource for CT African American genealogy.

Documents the African American experience in Connecticut from slavery through the Civil Rights era. Lists sites, churches, and communities associated with CT's Black history. Useful for identifying African American community records and church archives.

immigration_and_naturalization.description: Naturalization records from CT courts (Superior Court, Court of Common Pleas, District Court). CSL holds indexes and some original records; NARA Boston holds federal court naturalizations. Significant for Irish (1840s+), German (1850s+), Italian (1880s+), Polish (1890s+), and other immigrant groups who settled in CT's industrial cities.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Connecticut Immigrant Experience — Factory City Records
immigration_and_naturalization.repository: Various city libraries and historical societies
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Hartford (insurance/manufacturing), New Haven (manufacturing), Bridgeport (P.T. Barnum-era industry/WWI munitions), and Waterbury (brass) attracted massive immigration. City directories, factory records, ethnic organization records, and parish registers document these communities. Individual city libraries hold the strongest local collections.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Ancestry.com — Connecticut Passenger Lists and Immigration
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Most CT immigrants arrived through the Port of New York (Castle Garden / Ellis Island) and then traveled to CT. Search New York passenger lists for CT-bound passengers. Some also arrived via Boston or directly at New Haven. Ancestry provides indexed passenger lists and border crossings.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Connecticut has no burned counties. The state enjoys some of the best record survival in America, due to its town-level record keeping system (distributing records across 169 towns rather than concentrating them in 8 county courthouses), the early and systematic abstraction work of Lucius Barnes Barbour (vital records) and Charles R. Hale (cemetery records), and the Connecticut State Library's comprehensive microfilming program. The only significant wartime destruction was the British burning of Danbury (1777) and New London (1781), but in both cases critical town records had been evacuated beforehand.
